coredata.dc:title: Label-free electrochemical aptasensor for progesterone detection in biological fluids
coredata.dc:creator: Samie, Hedieh Asadi
Arvand, Majid
coredata.dc:description: A label-free electrochemical progesterone (P4) aptasensor was successfully developed by covalently immobilizing NH2-functionalized P4-specific aptamer on the electrode surface. The NiO-Au hybrid nanofibers were synthesized by the electrospinning technique. GQDs–NiO-AuNFs nanocomposite was prepared by dispersing of electrospun NiO-AuNFs in the as-synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) solution and stirring for 24 h. Novel GQDs–NiO-AuNFs nano-architecture in combination with functionalized multiwalled carbon nanotubes (f-MWCNTs) were further utilized to modify screen printed carbon electrode (SPCE) in order to construct an effective immobilization matrix with plenty of carboxylic functional groups. The stepwise assembly process of the designed aptasensor was characterized by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The aptamer-progesterone complex formation led to a hindered electron transfer reaction on the sensing interface, which decreased the redox probe peak current. Based on of this, progesterone could be quantitatively detected by monitoring the decrease of differential pulse voltammetric (DPV) responses of [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– peak current with increasing the progesterone concentration. Under optimized experimental parameters, the aptasensor exhibited a dynamic concentration range from 0.01 to 1000 nM and a detection limit of 1.86 pM. The proposed aptasensor was successfully employed for the determination of progesterone in human serum samples and pharmaceutical formulations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical aptasensor
Progesterone
Graphene quantum dots
Electrospun nanofibers
Screen printed carbon electrode

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical performance of the modified electrodes
Application of aptasensor for progesterone detection in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of GQDs at different magnifications, (B) FESEM image of Ni(NO3)2/HAuCl4/PVP nanofibers, (C) FESEM image of NiO-Au nanofibers, (D) TEM image of NiO-Au nanofibers, (E) and (F) FESEM images of GQDs–NiO-AuNFs nanocomposite.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of (a) SPCE, (b) f-MWCNTs/SPCE, (c) GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/SPCE, (d) GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs/SPCE, (e) aptamer/GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs/SPCE, (f) BSA/aptamer/GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs/SPCE and (g) P4/BSA/aptamer/GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs/SPCE in 0.1 M KCl containing 5.0 mM K3[Fe(CN)6], (B) Nyquist plots of (a) f-MWCNTs/SPCE, (b) GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs/SPCE, (c) aptamer/GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs/SPCE, (d) BSA/aptamer/GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs/SPCE and (e) P4/BSA/aptamer/GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs/SPCE in 0.1 M KCl containing 5.0 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] (inset: Nyquist plot of SPCE).
Optimization of experimental parameters: influence of (A) aptamer concentration, (B) aptamer immobilization time, (C) the reaction time of aptasensor with progesterone.

(A) DP voltammograms of BSA/aptamer/GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs for different concentrations of progesterone within 0.01–1000 nM, (B) calibration curve for ΔI and (C) ΔI vs. logC P4 (ΔI(μA) = 12.053logC P4 (nM) + 32.975; R 2 = 0.998).
Histogram of the change of aptasensor response after reaction with 1 nM progesterone and other hormones and proteins with a concentration of 100-fold higher than that of progesterone concentration.

DP voltammograms of BSA/aptamer/GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs containing serum sample before (dashed line) and after adding different amount of progesterone standard solutions to serum sample in 0.1 M KCl containing 5.0 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] (inset: calibration curve for ΔI vs. logC P4 (ΔI(μA) = 12.042logC P4 (nM) + 31.504; R 2 = 0.996)).
Schematic illustration of the preparation and working of the aptasensor proposed for progesterone detection.
Comparison of some characteristics of this aptasensor and other (bio)sensors previously reported for P4 detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: mAb: monoclonal antibody; BiFE: Bismuth film-plated electrode; SWAdSV: Square wave adsorptive stripping voltammetry; Anti-Prog: Monoclonal anti progesterone; Anti-Prog-Aucoll-graphite-Teflon: Anti-Prog-colloidal gold-graphite Teflon; PEDOT: Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxylthiopene); Anti-P4: Anti-human progesterone capture antibody; Protein G-MBs: Protein G functionalized-magnetic microbeads.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para:

± sx and sx denotes standard deviation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of P4 determination in the pharmaceutical products by DPV method (n = 4).

head.ce:title: Label-free electrochemical aptasensor for progesterone detection in biological fluids

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Guilan, Namjoo Street, P.O. Box: 1914, Rasht, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Guilan, Namjoo Street, P.O. Box: 1914, Rasht, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A label-free electrochemical progesterone (P4) aptasensor was successfully developed by covalently immobilizing NH2-functionalized P4-specific aptamer on the electrode surface. The NiO-Au hybrid nanofibers were synthesized by the electrospinning technique. GQDs–NiO-AuNFs nanocomposite was prepared by dispersing of electrospun NiO-AuNFs in the as-synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) solution and stirring for 24 h. Novel GQDs–NiO-AuNFs nano-architecture in combination with functionalized multiwalled carbon nanotubes (f-MWCNTs) were further utilized to modify screen printed carbon electrode (SPCE) in order to construct an effective immobilization matrix with plenty of carboxylic functional groups. The stepwise assembly process of the designed aptasensor was characterized by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The aptamer-progesterone complex formation led to a hindered electron transfer reaction on the sensing interface, which decreased the redox probe peak current. Based on of this, progesterone could be quantitatively detected by monitoring the decrease of differential pulse voltammetric (DPV) responses of [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– peak current with increasing the progesterone concentration. Under optimized experimental parameters, the aptasensor exhibited a dynamic concentration range from 0.01 to 1000 nM and a detection limit of 1.86 pM. The proposed aptasensor was successfully employed for the determination of progesterone in human serum samples and pharmaceutical formulations.

ce:section.ce:para: Progesterone (pregn-4-ene-3,20-dione, P4) is a 21 carbon hydrophobic steroid hormone secreted by the corpus luteum in the ovary, associated with the establishment and maintenance of pregnancy in mammals and essentially required for animal growth and development [1,2]. In the pharmaceutical industry, progesterone is utilized to regulate abnormal menstrual cycles and also plays a crucial role in menopausal hormone therapy and oral contraceptives [3]. Progesterone imbalance in the women organism may cause various side effects such as headache, body pain, tiredness, breast tenderness, stomach upset, diarrhea, constipation and urinal infections [4]. Consumption of high levels of progesterone in cow’s milk may result in lung and breast cancers and its high values also provoke negative effect on the gonadotropin releasing hormone secretion in males [5,6]. When high amount of progesterone is consumed, the body absorbs certain amount and the rest is discarded in the environment as waste. Accordingly, it is vital to monitor progesterone concentration in clinical and environmental samples [7]. Different analytical methods including high performance liquid chromatography [8], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [9] and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [10], time-resolved fluoroimmunoassay [11], fluorescent immunoassay [12], enzyme linked immunosorbent assay [13] and chemiluminescence enzyme immunoassay [14] have been employed to monitor progesterone concentration in both environmental and clinical samples. However, most of these methods have disadvantages; they are laborious, complicated, time-consuming and require sample preparation steps with expensive equipments. Thus, electrochemical methods can be substituted for the current analytical techniques due to their high selectivity, sensitivity, cost-effectiveness and reproducible results [15,16].

Aptamers are the artificial single-stranded DNA or RNA oligonucleotides which are isolated from random sequence nucleic acid libraries by an in vitro selection method termed systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment (SELEX) and have specific recognition capability toward certain given target molecules such as protein, drugs, toxicants, amino acids, vitamins and even whole cells [17,18]. Aptamers offer a powerful alternative to antibodies as biorecognition molecules [19]. Owning to the beneficial features of aptamers compared to other biorecognition elements such as high thermal and chemical stability, less immunogenicity, easy artificial synthesis, specificity, low cost, reusability and flexibility of chemical modification, aptasensors provide superior advantages over biosensors based on traditional antibodies or enzymes [20–23]. Among the various aptamer-based sensors, electrochemical aptasensors have become more preferable for the on-site and rapid analysis application due to their efficient advantages such as fast response time, high sensitivity and specificity, simple operation, more potential for miniaturization, portability and relatively low cost [24,25].

Nanomaterials with high surface to volume ratio and abundant binding sites have been extensively employed as signal amplifying molecules to increase the immobilized aptamer amount on the electrode surface, which has a tremendous impact on the electrochemical aptasensor performances such as their sensitivity, specificity and lifetime [26,27].

The inherent characteristics of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) such as large surface area, good electrical conductivity and unique electromechanical properties have made them appropriate to be used in electrochemical biosensing systems [28]. MWCNTs with their dual roles both as matrices for immobilization of biomolecules and transducers which relay the electrochemical signal have the potential for use in the electrochemical aptasensor [29]. In addition, MWCNTs can easily interact with other carbon nanomaterials such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through π-π stacking interaction because of their similar conjugated structures, resulting in the formation of a 3D network for the hybrid nanomaterial with exceptional stability, higher conductivity and faster electron transfer kinetics [30].

GQDs are zero-dimensional graphene sheets with characteristics derived from both graphene and carbon dots (CDs) and lateral dimensions less than 100 nm, which can offer unique properties in many aspects owning to their considerable quantum confinement and edge effects [31]. GQDs have been considered to be the most suitable matrices for the immobilization of biorecognition elements due to their incomparable properties including the presence of functional groups, large surface area to volume ratio, susceptibility to chemical modifications, good biocompatibility and enhanced electronic conductivity [32,33].

Recently, semiconductor-carbon heterostructure nanocomposites have received more attention because of their fascinating properties and extensive applicability in various fields, particularly in (bio)sensing systems [34]. The incorporation of carbon nanomaterials in conjunction with metal-oxide semiconductor nanostructures can provide novel nanocomposites with exotic properties arising from the synergistic effects, which is highly favorable for electrocatalytic applications [35]. Among the semiconducting transitional metal oxides, nickel oxide (NiO), a p-type semiconductor has emerged as promising nanomaterials in electrochemical sensor and biosensor fabrication due to its distinctive properties such as large surface to volume ratio, good biocompatibility, nontoxicity, high chemical stability and good electronic conductivity [36]. Among the metal oxide nanostructures, metal oxide nanofibers have several salient characteristics including excellent flexibility in surface functionalities, superior mechanical performance, abundant responsive sites, high porosity, relatively large specific surface area and greater ability of efficient catalysis compared with other geometric shape nanomaterials [37]. Moreover, compared to single metal oxides, nanocomposites doped with noble metals such as NiO-Au hybrid nanofibers show superior electrocatalytic activity than their monometallic counterparts due to the synergistic effect [38].

Motivated by the above mentioned, herein, we proposed a novel label-free electrochemical aptasensor based on GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs modified screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) for ultrasensitive progesterone detection in human serum samples and pharmaceutical formulations. As far as we know, such label-free electrochemical aptasensor for progesterone determination has not been reported. This aptasensor demonstrated good sensitivity, high selectivity and wide linear range with a very low detection limit for progesterone detection due to the specificity of aptamer as well as significant catalytic ability and large specific surface area of GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs nanocomposite which was capable of loading more aptamer and maintaining excellent properties for aptasensor.

Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and HAuCl4·3H2O were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP, MW = 1,300,000 g mol−1), citric acid monohydrate, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), human serum albumin (HSA), human immunoglobulin G (IgG) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, Missouri, USA) and used as received without further purification. The MWCNTs (purity > 95%, diameter 54 nm, length 1–10 μm, number of walls 3–15) were obtained from Nanostartech Co. (Tehran, Iran). Progesterone and other hormones were procured from Aburaihan Pharmaceutical Co (Tehran, Iran). NH2-functionalized P4-specific aptamer was provided from Bioneer Corporation (South Korea), with the sequence of 5′-NH2-C6-GCATCACACACCGATACTCACCCGCCTGATTAACATTAGCCCACCGCCCACCCCCGCTGC-3′, designed according to previous publication [39].

Electrochemical measurements were carried out using μAutolab PGSTAT 30 electrochemical analyzer (Ecochemie BV, Utrecht, the Netherlands) controlled by Nova 2.1 software. The screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) (3.3 × 1.0 × 0.05 cm, length × width × height) consists of a graphite working electrode, a graphite counter electrode and a silver pseudo reference electrode. The graphite working SPCE surface is 4 mm in diameter. These disposable SPCEs were obtained from DropSens (Spain). A homemade interface was employed for connecting the SPCEs to the electrochemical analyzer. All electrochemical studies were performed at 25 ± 1 °C. The pH of the solutions was adjusted by a model 827 pH Lab (Metrohm Swiss meter). A model CM10 TEM (Philips) and MIRA3 TESCAN-XMU FESEM were utilized to characterize the morphology, size and structure of the nanomaterials. The chemical composition was obtained using EDS (SAMx, France).

The GQDs were synthesized by directly pyrolyzing of citric acid (CA) following the previously reported procedure [40]. Briefly, 2 g CA was put into a round-bottomed flask and heated to 200 °C for about 30 min, until the colorless CA solids turned gradually into an orange liquid, indicating the formation of GQDs. Then, the orange liquid was adjusted to pH 7.0 by 20 mg mL−1 NaOH solution, under vigorous stirring. After the neutralization reaction with NaOH, the aqueous solution of GQDs was obtained.

NiO-AuNFs were prepared from Ni(NO3)2/HAuCl4/PVP composite nanofibers which were fabricated via the electrospinning process. In a typical synthesis, 1 g PVP was mixed with 3 mL ethanol under vigorous stirring for 1 h to obtain a homogeneous solution. Therewith, the other solution containing 0.6 g Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.1 g HAuCl4·3H2O and 1 mL water was added into the above solution. After further stirring for 1 h, the mixture was loaded into a plastic syringe with a 23-gauge stainless steel needle as nozzle and connected to high-voltage power supply. An electrostatic voltage of 16 kV was applied between the steel needle and a flat aluminum foil which was placed at a distance of 12 cm of the needle tip. The feeding rate of the precursor solution was adjusted using a homemade syringe pump and it was kept constant at 1.5 mL h−1. In order to remove polymer matrix completely, the precursory nanofibers were calcined in air atmosphere at 550 °C for 3.5 h with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1 [38].

GQDs–NiO-AuNFs nanocomposite was synthesized by dispersing 2.4 mg of electrospun NiO-AuNFs in 3.0 mL of the GQDs suspension and stirring for 24 h. Unbound GQDs were eliminated by successive centrifugation and the product was washed with deionized water thrice and then dried in air at room temperature to get the GQDs–NiO-AuNFs nanocomposite [30].

The SPCE surface was cleaned by sonication in ethanol and subsequently washed with deionized water, prior to its modification. The bare SPCE was coated with GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs as follows. The nanocomposite suspension was provided by dispersing 2 mg f-MWCNTs and 2 mg GQDs–NiO-AuNFs in 100 μL of DMF solvent under the ultrasonic agitation for 10 min, giving a black dispersion. 2 μL of the prepared suspension was carefully coated on the SPCE surface and dried in air at room temperature. To ensure that the screen-printed carbon electrode surface was completely covered by the nanocomposite, drop-casting process was performed two consecutive times. In order to immobilize the P4-aptamer, GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs/SPC modified electrode was incubated in the phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4) containing 150 mM EDC and 100 mM NHS to activate the terminal -COOH groups for 1 h. During this process, the -COOH groups present on the interface of the GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs composite were covalently attached by EDC, which then released 1-(3-dimethylamino) propyl-3-ethylurea after reacted with NHS. This structure is appropriate for the reaction of NH2-aptamer and GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs nanocomposite through covalent amide bonds between the amino groups of the aptamer and the carboxyl groups of the nanocomposite [28,41]. Briefly, the terminal -COOH groups were activated with a mixture of EDC and NHS and subsequently the specific aptamers were immobilized on the modified SPCE by amide bond formation. Afterward, the GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs/SPC modified electrode was incubated with PBS (pH 7.4) containing 5.0 μM NH2-functionalized P4-specific aptamer for 2 h at room temperature. The aptasensor was then immersed in 0.1% BSA solution for 30 min to cover the unspecific sites and prevent nonspecific adsorption. After this, in order to eliminate the redundant BSA on the electrode interface, the aptasensor was rinsed with PBS. Eventually, 100 μL of progesterone solutions with different concentrations were incubated onto the BSA/aptamer/GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs/SPC electrode. After thoroughly incubation, the aptasensor was rinsed with PBS to remove the uncombined progesterone on the sensing interface and obtain the P4/BSA/aptamer/GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs/SPCE for subsequent electrochemical detection (Schematic 1 ) [42].

The standard addition technique was used for the determination of P4 in real samples according to described procedures. Human blood serum samples were collected from healthy volunteers (serum 1: female, 32 years and serum 2: female, 41 years) at the Ashtiani Pathobiology Laboratory (Rasht, Iran) and stored frozen until assay. A 100 μL of each one of them was separately transferred into the 10 mL volumetric flask and diluted to the volume with 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.5). Then known volume of diluted human serum samples were spiked with different concentrations of P4 without further pretreatment. The spiked samples of biological fluids were completed to the calibration mark with the PBS (pH 7.5). The DPV measurements with BSA/aptamer/GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs/SPCE were performed after incubation of the aptasensor with these samples. Commercial injection solution and tablet samples of progesterone (Aburaihan Pharmaceutical Co.) were bought from a local pharmacy in Rasht, Iran. For P4 analysis, 0.5 mL of each P4 ampoule was transferred into a 50 mL volumetric flask and diluted with the selected supporting electrolyte and mixed. Then, known volume of this solution was diluted with PBS (pH 7.5) and analyzed with standard addition method as described in serum sample analysis. For tablet sample analysis, five tablets were accurately weighed and homogenized together by grinding to represent an average and fine powder. A portion of the powder, equivalent amount to one tablet was transferred to a 50 mL volumetric flask and dissolved in the mixture solution of ethanol-deionized water (1:1 v/v) and sonicated during 20 min at ambient temperature. The solution was subsequently filtered through Whatman 42 (125 mm diameter) filter paper to obtain a clear filtrate. Afterwards, 100 μL of the solution was attenuated with PBS (pH 7.5) and then used to determine P4 by DPV method.

The typical TEM was utilized to characterize the surface morphology of as-prepared GQDs. Fig. 1 A displays the TEM image of the synthesized GQDs and the inset was the related TEM image in higher magnification. According to the figure, uniform spherical shape of GQDs with the size ranging from 10 to 20 nm in diameter was clearly observed. In addition, the transparency of the GQDs indicates that most of the GQDs consisted of few layers of graphene [40,43]. Fig. S1A displays the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CA and GQDs. UV–Vis spectrum of the aqueous suspension of GQDs shows a broad absorption band at approximately 380 nm related to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains, which is not observed in the UV–Vis spectrum of CA. The FT-IR spectra of the GQDs and CA were recorded to obtain further structural information on the obtained GQDs. As shown in Fig. S1B, no characteristic absorption bands of aromatic compound are included in the GQDs spectrum, implying that there is no aromatic compound in the obtained GQDs. It should be noted also that the GQDs display nearly no absorption of CH, indicating complete carbonization of citric acid during synthesis. FT-IR spectrum of GQDs shows five characteristic peaks of citric acid, which correspond to the CO stretching vibration at 1680 cm−1, CC stretching vibration at 1590 cm−1, COH stretching vibration at 1380 cm−1 and the CO stretching vibration at 1090 cm−1. Additionally, the broad peak at around 3400 cm−1 is attributed to the OH stretching vibration from the carboxylic acid group in the GQDs. Therefore, the FT-IR results indicated the existence of various oxygen-containing functional groups (including carboxyl and hydroxyl groups) on the GQDs surface.

The morphology and structure of the obtained Ni(NO3)2/HAuCl4/PVP and NiO-Au composite nanofibers were investigated by FESEM. As can be seen from Fig. 1B the surfaces of as-electrospun Ni(NO3)2/HAuCl4/PVP nanofibers were uniform and smooth and no beads or other defects were observed in the nanofibers. After the calcination process, the flat and continuous nanofibers structures were still kept, while the surfaces became rough (Fig. 1C). Also, because of the evaporation of water and the nitrate group and the decomposition and removal of PVP during the calcination treatment, the nanofibers exhibited shrinkages and the average diameters were significantly decreased [38]. TEM images of the NiO-Au nanofibers (Fig. 1D) confirmed a highly porous structure of the nanofibers that composed of numerous nanoparticles, which possess large surface area and minimize transport hindrance for subsequent catalytic reactions [37]. Furthermore, EDS (Fig. S1C) revealed the existence of the element of Ni, O, and Au existing in the NiO-Au composite nanofibers. GQDs–NiO-AuNFs were subsequently synthesized by dispersing of electrospun NiO-AuNFs in the as-synthesized GQDs solution and stirring for 24 h. As can be seen from Fig. 1E and F, the relative coverage of the NiO-Au nanofibers with a layer of graphene quantum dots led to the formation of new nanocomposite with numerous edge-plane-like defective sites, which could increase electron transfer and thereby enhance the efficiency of the proposed nanocomposite. Accordingly, this nanocomposite allowed remarkable enhancement of the electrochemical signal and contributed to improving sensitivity. In addition, the existence of large amount of small-sized GQDs on the surface of the nanofibers, leading to increase defective interaction between the carboxyl-rich nanocomposite surface and amino-functionalized aptamer. The rough surface morphology of electrospun NiO-Au nanofibers could help depositing of graphene quantum dots on NiO-Au nanofibers. Fig. S1D shows the EDS elemental analysis of the representative GQDs–NiO-AuNFs nanocomposite surface. As shown in this figure the characteristic elements of this nanostructure, such as Ni, O, Au and C were observed, confirming the successful coating of GQDs on NiO-AuNFs and the preparation of the GQDs–NiO-AuNFs nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of the modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The stepwise assembly process of the aptasensor was characterized by CV in 0.1 M KCl containing 5.0 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]. As shown in Fig. 2 A, a pair of redox peaks with an anodic peak current (I pa) of 24.6 μA and a peak potential separation (ΔE p) of 0.281 V was observed at the surface of SPCE. After modification of the SPCE surface with f-MWCNTs, the signal was considerably intensified and ΔE p was significantly reduced (I pa = 76.08, ΔE p = 0.090). At the surface of GQDs–NiO-AuNFs modified screen-printed carbon electrode I pa = 84.2 μA and ΔE p = 0.082 V was observed. After further modification of electrode with f-MWCNTs and GQDs–NiO-AuNFs, the peak current further increased while ΔE p further decreased (I pa = 115.77 μA, ΔE p = 0.071 V), demonstrating that this kind of nanocomposite greatly increased the electrode surface area and synergistically accelerated electron transfer between the electrode and [Fe(CN)6]3–/4–. The strong hydrogen bonding and π–π stacking interactions operating between GQDs and MWCNTs make a 3D network for the hybrid material and possess both the high charge density of graphene and 3D network of MWCNTs with large surface area. When the NH2-aptamer was covalently immobilized on the GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs/SPCE surface, a decreased redox peak current and an increased ΔE p was obtained (I pa = 70.07 μA, ΔE p = 0.134 V). This was due to electrostatic repulsion between the negatively charged phosphate backbone of the immobilized aptamer on the modified SPCE surface and [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– redox probe molecule. To block the unspecific sites on the aptasensor interface, the aptamer/GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs modified screen-printed carbon electrode was incubated in BSA for 30 min. Accumulation of BSA on the surface of the electrode effectively blocked the active sites necessary for electron transfer; that led to a decrease in the redox peak current and an increase in the peak separation (I pa = 62.05 μA, ΔE p = 0.158 V) [18]. After the incubation of aptasensor with progesterone, the peak current further decreased while ΔE p increased (I pa = 51.28 μA, ΔE p = 0.195 V), indicating the conformational changes associated with aptamer during its interaction with the progesterone target molecule.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was also utilized to investigate the stepwise changes in the electrochemical surface properties of the electrodes. The impedance spectrum is known as Nyquist plot, include a semicircle part at higher frequencies representing to the charge-transfer resistance (R ct) and a linear part at lower frequencies corresponding the diffusion limited process [44]. Fig. 2B illustrates the Nyquist plots of EIS spectrum after each medication step which were recorded in the [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– as a redox probe. According to the Fig. 2B, a large semicircle corresponded to the impedance of the bare SPCE indicated a high electron transfer resistance (3386 Ω). At the f-MWCNTs modified screen-printed carbon electrode, the R ct and semicircle domain has dramatically decreased, demonstrating the great electrical conductivity of the f-MWCNTs (259 Ω). After further modification with GQDs–NiO-AuNFs, the electrode displayed an almost straight line in the Nyquist plot of impedance spectroscopy, which implied very low electron transfer resistance and the enhancement of the conductivity (124 Ω). As mentioned above, these remarkable improvements in electrochemical responses could be attributed to the expansion of the available surface area of the modified electrode. With immobilization of aptamer on the electrode surface, the interfacial electron-transfer resistance and thereby the diameter of the semicircle (R ct) increased significantly owing to the blocking effect of the negatively charged phosphate backbone of aptamer that inhibit charge transfer at the electrode surface and increase electrostatic repulsion between negative surface charges of aptamer and [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– redox probe (457 Ω). Adding non-conductive BSA as blocking agent could effectively passivate the defects in the immobilized DNA aptamer layer on the electrode surface, which caused the resistance increase again (718 Ω). The surface passivation with BSA could be able to eliminate nonspecific adsorptions in the subsequent aptasensor construction processes and force vertical alignment of the immobilized aptamer molecules, facilitating effective binding of the aptamers to progesterone [45]. After the use of aptasensor to detect progesterone, the highly significant hindrance effect of the aptamer-progesterone complex interrupted the electrolyte [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– transduction which led to the dramatic increase in semicircle diameter and R ct (832 Ω). Therefore, the data obtained using EIS technique was in good agreement with those obtained by utilizing CV method. The results all above confirm that the sensing interface has been fabricated successfully.

In order to achieve the best analytical performance of P4-aptasensor, the experimental conditions were optimized in terms of aptamer concentration, aptamer immobilization time and the reaction time of aptasensor with progesterone. Firstly, the effect of aptamer concentration for immobilization was investigated by varying its concentration (from 1.0 to 10.0 μM) on the GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs/SPCE and then evaluated the DPV response. From Fig. 3 A, it can be seen that the changes in the DPV peak currents (ΔI = I 0–I, where I 0 and I was the current value of the aptasensor before and after incubation with progesterone) evidently increased with the increasing of aptamer concentration from 1.0 to 5.0 μM. The change of peak current was not clear when the aptamer concentration gradually increased from 5.0 to 10.0 μM. This suggested that the active immobilizing sites on the surface of GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs nanocomposite were completely saturated with 5.0 μM of aptamer and therefore, this concentration could be regarded as the optimal aptamer concentration in the following experiment. The effect of incubation time for aptamer immobilization was also studied. The GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs/SPCEs were separately incubated in 5.0 μM of aptamer solution for different times and then undergone DPV detection. As presented in Fig. 3B, the electrochemical signal reached a plateau after 120 min. Therefore, 120 min was chosen as the optimal aptamer immobilization time. The reaction time of progesterone with its aptamer is another essential factor that influenced the aptasensor sensitivity. Accordingly, the influence of the reaction time of aptasensor and progesterone on the DPV response of [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– was also optimized. As displayed in Fig. 3C, the ΔI value of the aptasensor (incubated with 10 nM of progesterone) obviously increased with increasing the reaction time from 10 min to 60 min and then reached a maximum value in 60 min, but no significant increase in ΔI was observed after this time. This result demonstrated that the formation of the aptamer-progesterone complex was completed after 60 min.

The analytical performance of the prepared aptasensor was evaluated under the optimal experimental conditions. For this purpose, the fabricated aptasensor was incubated with various concentrations of progesterone in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution and subsequently the DPV responses of aptasensor were detected in 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]. As shown in Fig. 4 A, with the increasing of progesterone concentration, the DPV peak current gradually decreased because of the configuration change of the aptamer and formation of 3D-complex between aptamer and progesterone on the electrode interface that caused inhibition of the electron transfer of redox probe. Indeed, the captured progesterone molecules on the aptasensor interface, as a blocking layer, could hinder the electrochemical reaction and consequently decrease the DPV peak current. Based on of this, progesterone could be quantitatively detected by monitoring the decrease of DPV response of the aptasensor. Variation of the aptasensor response against the progesterone concentrations in the range of 0.01–1000 nM are demonstrated in Fig. 4B. A logarithmic correlation between the changes in the DPV peak currents (ΔI) and progesterone concentration (C P4) was seen. There was a well linear relationship between ΔI (μA) and logC P4 (nM) in the range of 0.01–1000 nM with the linear regression equation of ΔI (μA) = 12.053logC P4 (nM) + 32.975 and correlation coefficient of 0.998, as shown in Fig. 4C. The calculated limit of detection (LOD) was 1.86 pM and the limit of quantification was determined to be 6.21 pM. To clarify the fabricated aptasensor superiority toward progesterone detection, the performance characteristics of the proposed aptasensor was compared to those of other progesterone (bio)sensors and the results were listed in Table 1 . By achieving broad linear range and also a very low detection limit, the proposed aptasensor exhibits better analytical performance than previously studied progesterone (bio)sensors. Such superior sensing properties may be attributed to the following advantages: (i) high conductivity and fast electron-transfer kinetics which is due to the synergistic effect between GQDs–NiO-AuNFs and f-MWCNTs; (ii) large surface-to-volume ratio in the presence of GQDs–NiO-AuNFs and f-MWCNTs; (iii) the existence of more hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups in synthetic GQDs and f-MWCNTs, which is helpful for orderly bonding with more NH2-functionalized aptamer; (iv) block the unspecified sites on the aptasensor interface by BSA to prevent non-specific adsorption and reduce the detection background; (v) the simple label-free fabrication and detection process.

To assess the selectivity of the proposed aptasensor, other interferential hormones and proteins that may coexist with progesterone in biological samples were chosen for evaluation. The DPV response of aptasensor in the absence and presence of progesterone, testosterone, hydrocortisone, estradiol, estriol, human serum albumin and human immunoglobulin G was evaluated. It was clearly observed that a notable decrease of the DPV signals was induced by adding 1 nM of progesterone, whereas the incubation of the aptasensor with other common hormones and proteins with a concentration of 100-fold higher than that of progesterone concentration displayed no remarkable change of DPV signals. As obvious in Fig. 5 , current change (ΔI) of the aptasensor for progesterone was more evident than those of the other interfering substances which were negligible. Therefore, the developed aptasensor was highly specific towards its target hormone against other hormones and proteins. This feature could be attributed to the highly specific and strong interaction between aptamer and progesterone.

To estimate the fabrication reproducibility of proposed aptasensor, five parallel-prepared BSA/aptamer/GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs/SPCEs were utilized to detect 1 nM progesterone by DPV. The obtained relative standard deviation (RSD) was 4.2%, suggesting that the fabrication of the designed aptasensor was reproducible. The long term stability of the developed aptasensor was also investigated by detecting its DPV current responses. The proposed aptasensor was kept in the refrigerator with 4 °C temperature and used for determination of 1 nM progesterone. After an interval of one week, the aptasensor response current decreased to 94.7% and also the aptasensor retained 91.8% of its initial activity after a 30-day storage period, implying the desirable stability. The good stability of the designed aptasensor could be attributed to high stability of GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs nanocomposite as an integrated sensing interface and complete immobilization of aptamer on the surface of this nanocomposite through covalent bonding.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of aptasensor for progesterone detection in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the applicability and reliability of the proposed aptasensor for real samples analysis, BSA/aptamer/GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs modified screen-printed carbon electrode was used for the detection of progesterone in human serum samples and pharmaceutical formulations by standard addition method. For this purpose, 100 μL of human serum samples were diluted to 10 mL with PBS and subsequently different amounts of progesterone were added to the diluted samples. Fig. 6 shows the representative DPVs for the detection of progesterone in the human serum sample. The results are reported in Table 2 . The obtained results for detection of progesterone in human serum samples using the proposed aptasensor were compared with the reference values that obtained by electrochemiluminescence (ECL) method, by applying the statistical calculations (paired t-test and F-test). At the 95% confidence level, the calculated (experimental) values of paired t-test and F-test were less than their tabulated (theoretical) values, implying that there was no noticeable difference between the performance of the two compared methods regarding precision and accuracy. The results obtained from the analysis of pharmaceutical samples were summarized in Table 3 . The satisfactory and acceptable relative standard deviation and recovery values implied that the designed method had great accuracy and precision and also revealed that the developed electrochemical aptasensor had considerable potential in the practical sample analysis.

In this work, simple and rapid label-free electrochemical aptasensor for ultrasensitive determination of progesterone was constructed by covalent attachment of NH2-functionalized aptamer on the GQDs–NiO-AuNFs/f-MWCNTs nanocomposite as an outstanding transduction platform. The label-free method is simple and beneficial for keeping more active sites on the sensing interface. The application of aptamer, owing to its beneficial features, not only can enhance the selectivity but also improve the stability of the biosensor. The developed aptasensor has demonstrated a great potential for the actual determination of progesterone in clinical and diagnostic applications because of the advantages of straight forward operation, simple configuration and time efficiency. This innovative fabrication strategy for the development of label-free electrochemical aptasensor as portable device would be a promising approach in fast and precise detection of various analytes in the near future. However, some issues are still remaining to be addressed in the field of aptasensor application as an analytical approach. Simultaneous detection of different analytes in one sample would be one of the most challenging concerns. Development of such aptasensors capable of rapid and simultaneous detection of the small amounts of different analytes in biological and clinical samples will be the most outstanding perspective in this area.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors confirm that this article content has no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the post-graduate office of Guilan University for the support of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bioelechem.2020.107489.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
